message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (for a code change the named test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if reverted; for a doc/decision item, judge whether the documented claim is now accurate/honest). Repo /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix H-002 in src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
What: `powered` recorded in verdict evidence + documented as a trusted caller obligation.
Test(s): test_powered_flag_is_recorded_in_evidence
Read the actual changed file(s); read the test; check no regression. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN. Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify the H-002 fix. Let me start by examining the changed file and test.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git log --oneline -5 && echo "---" && grep -rn "powered" src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
input.description: Show recent commits and powered references

af29d19 Merge pull request #162 from [REDACTED]/harden/pass-4

---
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:107:    powered: bool,
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:113:    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:127:    ``powered`` is likewise a TRUSTED CALLER OBLIGATION (pass 5, #H-002): the engine cannot
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:129:    test was adequately powered (``powered=False`` forces ``indeterminate``). The engine cannot
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:136:    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:143:            "powered": powered,  # trusted caller flag, recorded for audit (#H-002)
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:146:    if not powered:
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:147:        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:163:    powered: bool,
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:213:        powered=powered,

1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	import warnings
29	from typing import Iterable, Sequence, cast
30	
31	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
32	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
33	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
34	    TimestampAuthority,
35	    require_preregistered,
36	)
37	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
38	
39	
40	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
41	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
42	
43	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
44	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
45	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
46	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
47	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
48	    """
49	    if authority is not None:
50	        require_preregistered(
51	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
52	        )
53	    else:
54	        # Falling back to the cheap presence gate means a hand-set timestamp_proof string passes
55	        # WITHOUT any cryptographic verification. Warn so a direct caller cannot silently skip real
56	        # pre-registration (#G-006); the runners always supply an authority and never hit this.
57	        warnings.warn(
58	            "confirm primitive called with authority=None: pre-registration is verified by a "
59	            "PRESENCE check only (lock fields populated), NOT cryptographically. Pass a "
60	            "TimestampAuthority to verify the proof binds the hypothesis and precedes confirmation.",
61	            stacklevel=3,
62	        )
63	        require_locked(hypothesis)
64	
65	
66	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
67	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
68	
69	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
70	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
71	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
72	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
73	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
74	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
75	    """
76	    if not hypothesis.locked:
77	        raise FirewallViolation(
78	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
79	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
80	        )
81	
82	
83	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
84	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
85	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
86	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
87	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
88	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
89	
90	
91	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
92	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
93	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
94	
95	
96	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
97	    if not math.isfinite(value):
98	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
99	
100	
101	def verdict_from_pvalue(
102	    hypothesis_id: str,
103	    *,
104	    statistic: float,
105	    p_value: float,
106	    alpha: float,
107	    powered: bool,
108	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
109	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
110	) -> Verdict:
111	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
112	
113	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
114	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
115	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
116	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
117	
118	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
119	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
120	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
121	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
122	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
123	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
124	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
125	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
126	
127	    ``powered`` is likewise a TRUSTED CALLER OBLIGATION (pass 5, #H-002): the engine cannot
128	    compute statistical power from an opaque statistic/p-value, so the caller asserts whether the
129	    test was adequately powered (``powered=False`` forces ``indeterminate``). The engine cannot
130	    verify it but records it in ``evidence`` so the verdict's dependence on the flag is auditable.
131	    """
132	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
133	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
134	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
135	
136	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
137	    common = dict(
138	        statistic=statistic,
139	        threshold=alpha,
140	        evidence={
141	            "p_value": p_value,
142	            "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim,
143	            "powered": powered,  # trusted caller flag, recorded for audit (#H-002)
144	        },
145	    )
146	    if not powered:
147	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
148	    if p_value <= alpha:
149	        if direction_supports_claim:
150	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
151	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
152	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
153	
154	
155	def confirm_unit_level(
156	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
157	    *,
158	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
159	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
160	    statistic: float,
161	    p_value: float,
162	    alpha: float,
163	    powered: bool,
164	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
165	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
166	) -> Verdict:
167	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
168	
169	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
170	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
171	
172	    Direction (pass 4, #G-017): unlike :func:`confirm_whole_corpus` — where the verdict polarity
173	    is derived from the pre-registered ``predicted_direction`` tail — the unit-level verdict is
174	    governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied ``direction_supports_claim`` flag (the engine sees only
175	    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
176	    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
177	    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
178	    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
179	
180	    Consequently (pass 5, #H-015) the unit-level path deliberately does NOT mirror
181	    :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`'s directionless-lock REFUSAL: because the locked direction never
182	    drives the unit-level verdict, a directionless lock here is harmless, not a HARKing vector —
183	    the asymmetry is intentional, not a missing guard.
184	
185	    Test identity (pass 5, #H-003): ``test_name`` is bound into the pre-registration proof for
186	    audit, but the engine receives only an opaque ``statistic``/``p_value`` and CANNOT verify that
187	    the test actually executed matches the locked ``test_name`` — that remains a caller obligation.
188	    """
189	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
190	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
191	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
192	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
193	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
194	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
195	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
196	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
197	    # against an unrecognized tail.
198	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
199	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
200	    ):
201	        raise ValueError(
202	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
203	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
204	        )
205	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
206	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
207	    alpha = cast(float, _resolve_locked_param(hypothesis.alpha, alpha, "alpha"))  # #H-001
208	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
209	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
210	        statistic=statistic,
211	        p_value=p_value,
212	        alpha=alpha,
213	        powered=powered,
214	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
215	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
216	    )
217	
218	
219	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
220	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
221	    n = len(null)
222	    if tail == "greater":
223	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
224	    else:
225	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
226	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
227	
228	
229	def _resample_stability(
230	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
231	) -> float:
232	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
233	
234	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
235	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
236	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
237	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
238	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
239	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
240	
241	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
242	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
243	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
244	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
245	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
246	    """
247	    n = len(null)
248	    try:
249	        import numpy as np
250	
251	        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
252	        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
253	        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
254	        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
255	        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
256	        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
257	        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
258	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
259	        if tail == "greater":
260	            extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="left")
261	        else:
262	            extreme = np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="right")
263	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
264	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
265	    except ImportError:
266	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
267	        return significant / len(resamples)
268	
269	
270	def _resolve_locked_param(locked: float | None, supplied: float | None, name: str) -> float | None:
271	    """Cross-check a confirm-time threshold against the pre-registered one (pass 5, #H-001).
272	
273	    If the hypothesis locked the value, the confirm-time argument may only AGREE with it (a
274	    mismatch raises) — the analyst cannot pick alpha / the stability bar after seeing the data
275	    (threshold HARKing). The locked value wins. If nothing was locked, the supplied value stands
276	    (exploratory / opted-out use), mirroring how direction worked before it could be locked.
277	    """
278	    if locked is not None:
279	        if supplied is not None and supplied != locked:
280	            raise ValueError(
281	                f"{name} argument {supplied!r} contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
282	                f"{name} {locked!r} — the threshold is fixed at lock time (no post-hoc choice)"
283	            )
284	        return locked
285	    return supplied
286	
287	
288	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
289	
290	
291	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
292	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time argument is
293	    optional and, if given, must MATCH the pre-registered one — it can cross-check, never supply.
294	
295	    A hypothesis reaching here has already passed the confirm gate, so it is locked and its
296	    ``predicted_direction`` is part of the cryptographic binding. A ``None`` locked direction
297	    therefore means the tail was *not* pre-registered; accepting a confirm-time direction in that
298	    case would let the analyst pick the tail AFTER seeing the data (HARKing), flipping
299	    supported↔contradicted on a hypothesis whose lock attests "no direction" (audit pass 4,
300	    #G-001). The tail must be committed before confirmation, so a directionless locked hypothesis
301	    is refused outright rather than completed with a post-hoc direction.
302	
303	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}``; any other value is
304	    rejected (an unrecognized tail would silently fall through to 'less' in :func:`_empirical_p`,
305	    flipping the verdict — audit pass 2, #128).
306	    """
307	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
308	    if locked_dir is None:
309	        raise ValueError(
310	            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
311	            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
312	            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
313	            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
314	        )
315	    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
316	        raise ValueError(
317	            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
318	            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
319	        )
320	    resolved = locked_dir
321	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
322	        raise ValueError(
323	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
324	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
325	        )
326	    return resolved
327	
328	
329	def confirm_whole_corpus(
330	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
331	    *,
332	    observed: float,
333	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
334	    alpha: float,
335	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
336	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,
337	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,
338	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
339	) -> Verdict:
340	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
341	
342	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
343	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
344	
345	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
346	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
347	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
348	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
349	
350	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
351	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
352	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
353	
354	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
355	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
356	    """
357	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
358	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
359	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
360	    # Cross-check the confirm-time thresholds against any pre-registered ones (#H-001): a locked
361	    # alpha / stability bar cannot be overridden post-hoc.
362	    alpha = cast(float, _resolve_locked_param(hypothesis.alpha, alpha, "alpha"))
363	    stability_threshold = cast(
364	        float,
365	        _resolve_locked_param(
366	            hypothesis.stability_threshold, stability_threshold, "stability_threshold"
367	        ),
368	    )
369	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
370	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
371	    if not null_distribution:
372	        raise ValueError(
373	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
374	        )
375	    for v in null_distribution:
376	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
377	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
378	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
379	
380	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
381	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
382	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
383	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
384	    n = len(null_distribution)
385	
386	    stability: float | None = None
387	    contra_stability: float | None = None
388	    if resample_statistics is not None:
389	        if not resample_statistics:
390	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
391	        for r in resample_statistics:
392	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
393	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
394	        # Also measure how consistently the resamples reproduce the OPPOSITE-tail significance, so a
395	        # contradiction's robustness is recorded in evidence even though (by design) a contradiction
396	        # is reportable without it (#G-005).
397	        contra_stability = _resample_stability(
398	            resample_statistics, null_distribution, opposite, alpha
399	        )
400	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
401	
402	    rule = (
403	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
404	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
405	    )
406	    evidence = {
407	        "p_support": p_support,
408	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
409	        "null_n": n,
410	        "predicted_direction": direction,
411	        "stability": stability,
412	        "contradiction_stability": contra_stability,
413	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
414	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
415	    }
416	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
417	
418	    # A contradiction (significant in the OPPOSITE tail) is reportable without a stability gate, and
419	    # this asymmetry with `supported` is INTENTIONAL (#G-005): support is a positive claim that must
420	    # reproduce to be decisive, whereas a statistic landing significantly in the wrong tail is
421	    # evidence OF the opposite — not absence of evidence — so it stands on the observed effect.
422	    # `contradiction_stability` is recorded in evidence (when resamples were supplied) so an auditor
423	    # can see whether the contradiction also reproduces, even though it is not gated on it.
424	    if p_contra <= alpha:
425	        return Verdict.contradicted(
426	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
427	        )
428	    if stability is None:
429	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
430	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
431	            rule,
432	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
433	            **common,
434	        )
435	    if not stable:
436	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
437	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
438	        )
439	    if p_support <= alpha:
440	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
441	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
442	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
443	    )
444

content.text:
All 43 tests pass. Assessment complete.

**Appropriate:** Line 143 records `powered` in the verdict's `evidence` dict, alongside the existing `p_value` and `direction_supports_claim` flags — consistent and idiomatic with the surrounding code. The gating behavior (line 146-147, `powered=False` → `indeterminate`) was already present and is now documented. The docstring (lines 127-130) accurately and honestly frames `powered` as a TRUSTED CALLER OBLIGATION the engine cannot verify but records for audit — matching the actual code behavior. The `rule` string also now includes `powered={powered}` (line 136). No regression: full file passes 43/43.

**Complete / discriminating:** The test checks both the recording (`evidence["powered"]` for True and False) and the gating (`powered=False` → INDETERMINATE). Reverting the recording line produces `KeyError: 'powered'` — confirmed the test fails when the fix is reverted.

input.reasoning:
Fix verified in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py. Line 143 records `"powered": powered` in the verdict's evidence dict (alongside p_value and direction_supports_claim), and lines 127-130 document `powered` as a TRUSTED CALLER OBLIGATION the engine cannot verify but records for audit; line 136 also surfaces it in the human-readable rule string. The docstring is accurate and honest: the engine genuinely cannot compute power from an opaque statistic/p-value, and `powered=False` does force `indeterminate` (lines 146-147), matching the documented claim.

Appropriate: idiomatic and consistent with the existing evidence-recording pattern; no regression — full tests/test_confirm.py passes 43/43 via the project venv.

Complete/discriminating: test_powered_flag_is_recorded_in_evidence (tests/test_confirm.py:561) asserts both the recording (evidence["powered"] is True/False) and the gating (powered=False → VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE). I confirmed it discriminates by deleting line 143 and rerunning: the test fails with KeyError: 'powered' (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py __getitem__). File restored to original after the experiment. Note: pytest is only installed in the .venv, not system python3 — the discriminating run used .venv/bin/python.

The fix records the `powered` flag in verdict evidence (line 143) and documents it as a trusted caller obligation in the docstring (lines 127-130), and the test discriminates (fails with KeyError when the recording line is reverted).
